title: Ileocecal Intussusception in 14-Month-Old with Air Enema Reduction
subtitle: Intermittent Pain + Bilious Vomiting + Currant-Jelly Stool Triad, Ultrasound 'Target Sign' Diagnosis, Air Enema Reduction First-Line (80–90% Success), Surgical Reduction if Perforation / Failed Enema / Pathologic Lead Point

patient.name: K. Olsen (Kai, 14 mo)
patient.admitDate: Today, 0830 (ED via parents; 18-hr history of intermittent severe abdominal pain with calm periods, bilious vomiting, and now blood/mucus per rectum)
patient.room: Pediatric ED Bed 4 → Peds Floor pending air enema in IR

vitals.time: 1030 (CURRENT — between pain episodes, calm; after IV fluids + ondansetron)

labs.v: 11.2 kg (50th percentile for age 14 mo); dosing weight for all medications + fluids; height 78 cm
labs.r: PEDIATRIC PRINCIPLE: ALL doses calculated per kg. Confirm at admission; document. Use for maintenance fluids (Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 = 4 mL/kg/hr first 10 kg + 2 mL/kg/hr next 10 = 40 + 2.4 = 42 mL/hr) + medications

labs.v: Age 1 yr normal: HR 90–150, RR 24–40, BP systolic 70–100, MAP ≥55; crying raises HR + RR significantly; tachycardia + tachypnea at REST = concerning
labs.r: Pediatric vital signs vary by age. Crying disrupts measurement — reassess when calm. Tachycardia at rest = concern for pain, dehydration, sepsis, hypovolemia. Hypotension is a LATE sign in peds shock

labs.n: CLASSIC TRIAD of intussusception (the diagnostic anchor)
labs.v: (1) INTERMITTENT SEVERE ABDOMINAL PAIN: episodes of inconsolable crying with drawing knees up to chest lasting 10–15 min, separated by CALM PERIODS where child appears comfortable; pattern began 18 hr ago, escalating in frequency from q1h initially to now q20 min; (2) VOMITING: initially non-bilious (gastric content), now BILIOUS (bright green/yellow — concerning sign suggesting obstruction); 8 episodes total; (3) STOOL: passed normal stool x 2 yesterday; today passed BLOODY MUCUSY STOOL ('currant jelly' appearance — mixed blood + mucus from sloughed intestinal mucosa) x 1 episode at 0700
labs.r: CLASSIC TRIAD for intussusception: INTERMITTENT PAIN + VOMITING + CURRANT-JELLY STOOL. Full triad present in only ~20% of cases — partial presentations more common. PAIN PATTERN is highly characteristic (severe episodes + calm intervals; child often lethargic between episodes). BILIOUS vomiting suggests obstruction (proximal to ampulla — distal obstruction). CURRANT JELLY = bowel ischemia → mucosal sloughing → blood + mucus — LATE sign (suggests significant ischemia)

labs.v: SAUSAGE-SHAPED MASS palpable in RIGHT UPPER QUADRANT (subtle, ~3 cm) — pathognomonic for intussusception when present (but only palpable ~50% of cases); right lower quadrant feels 'empty' (Dance's sign — telescoped bowel pulled from RLQ); abdomen mildly distended; mild diffuse tenderness with guarding during pain episodes, soft/non-tender between episodes; bowel sounds DECREASED but present; NO peritoneal signs (no rebound, no rigidity); rectal exam: mucusy + blood-tinged stool, no impacted feces, no obvious mass on palpation (intussusceptum may extend to anorectal canal in extreme cases — RECTAL PROLAPSE of intussusceptum if very distal)
labs.r: PHYSICAL FINDINGS of intussusception: sausage-shaped mass (RUQ — pathognomonic but ~50% sensitivity), Dance's sign (empty RLQ from telescoped bowel pulled cephalad), abdominal distention, decreased bowel sounds. PERITONEAL signs (rebound, rigidity, board-like abdomen, severe tenderness) = PERFORATION/PERITONITIS = SURGICAL EMERGENCY (no enema reduction; OR for surgical repair)

labs.v: Bedside US by peds emergency physician at 0900: 'TARGET SIGN' on axial view (concentric rings of telescoped bowel) in RUQ; 'PSEUDOKIDNEY SIGN' on longitudinal view (resembles kidney with hyperechoic mucosa and hypoechoic muscular layers); ILEOCECAL intussusception confirmed; minimal free fluid; NO perforation seen; no obvious pathologic lead point identified (idiopathic intussusception — most common in this age)
labs.r: ABDOMINAL ULTRASOUND is FIRST-LINE for intussusception (sensitivity + specificity >97%; non-invasive; no radiation; available bedside in many peds EDs); 'TARGET SIGN' (transverse) + 'PSEUDOKIDNEY SIGN' (longitudinal) are characteristic. Abdominal X-ray less sensitive (~50%); may show paucity of gas in RLQ, soft tissue mass, signs of obstruction. CT for older children or atypical cases; rarely needed in classic peds presentation

labs.v: PROXIMAL bowel (ileum) telescoping INTO DISTAL bowel (cecum/colon) = ILEOCECAL most common in children (~90%); other types: ileoileal, colocolic, ileoileocolic; intussusceptum (proximal) drags with it its mesentery → vascular compression → bowel wall edema + congestion → mucosal ischemia (sloughs → blood + mucus = currant jelly) → can progress to full-thickness necrosis → PERFORATION → peritonitis → sepsis
labs.r: TIME-SENSITIVE process. Earlier reduction (<24 hr from onset) = higher success of air enema + lower perforation risk. >48 hr or signs of perforation/sepsis = surgical reduction with possible bowel resection. Patient at 18 hr from onset — within window for air enema reduction trial

labs.v: Planned in INTERVENTIONAL RADIOLOGY (or peds GI) with continuous fluoroscopic monitoring; air insufflated rectally to pressure ~80–120 mmHg (institutional protocol; some use saline/contrast hydrostatic enema); reduction success defined by air passing freely into terminal ileum (telescoped segment relieved); SUCCESS RATE 80–90%; FAILURE → surgical reduction; PERFORATION risk ~1% (less than barium); ANESTHESIA: typically procedural sedation (per institutional + patient stability) or general anesthesia for high-risk cases; pediatric surgery on standby for failed enema or perforation
labs.r: AIR ENEMA preferred over HYDROSTATIC (saline/water-soluble contrast) ENEMA — less mess if perforation occurs, equivalent efficacy. BARIUM AVOIDED if perforation possible (chemical peritonitis from extravasated barium). Procedural sedation per institutional protocol; some centers use awake reduction in stable children. Surgery on standby; transfer to OR if perforation or failed reduction

labs.n: Indications for SURGICAL reduction (not air enema)
labs.v: Surgical reduction (laparoscopic or open) is indicated if: (a) AIR ENEMA FAILED (after 2–3 attempts per institutional protocol); (b) signs of PERFORATION or PERITONITIS at presentation (rigid abdomen, severe tenderness, free air on imaging, fever, sepsis); (c) HEMODYNAMIC INSTABILITY (shock); (d) PATHOLOGIC LEAD POINT identified (Meckel diverticulum, polyp, lymphoma — more common in older children >5 yr or recurrent intussusception); (e) BOWEL ISCHEMIA / NECROSIS suspected — may require resection of involved segment
labs.r: SURGICAL indications. Open laparotomy traditionally; LAPAROSCOPIC increasingly used at experienced centers. Manual reduction first; resection if non-viable bowel; primary anastomosis usually possible if resection limited. Risk of post-operative ileus, infection, recurrence (5–10% with surgery, similar with enema)

labs.v: Mild to moderate dehydration: capillary refill 2–3 sec (mildly prolonged); skin tenting +; dry mucous membranes; decreased tears with crying; UOP per parents 'less than usual' (last wet diaper 5 hr ago — typical 4 wet/day); abdominal exam consistent with obstruction (mild distention, decreased BS, vomiting); IV access established (22 g R hand); NS 20 mL/kg = 220 mL bolus given at 0900 (PALS recommendation for moderate dehydration); BMP results: Na 136, K 3.6, Cl 100, HCO3 22, BUN 14, Cr 0.4 (age-appropriate); glucose 88; mild metabolic alkalosis from vomiting
labs.r: Pediatric dehydration assessment: capillary refill (most sensitive in peds), mucous membranes, tears, fontanelle (still palpable at 14 mo), skin turgor, mental status, UOP. NS or LR 20 mL/kg bolus over 10–20 min for moderate dehydration; reassess; repeat if needed up to 60 mL/kg in first hour. Holliday-Segar maintenance after rehydration

labs.v: Between episodes: ALERT but lethargic (less playful than usual per parents); during pain episodes: inconsolable crying with knees drawn up; recognizes parents; following with eyes; NO altered mental status (rules out severe sepsis at present)
labs.r: INTUSSUSCEPTION LETHARGY can be profound (sometimes mistaken for sepsis or AMS); may be from pain, vomiting, dehydration, or as a primary feature ('intussusception lethargy' is well-described). Some children present with predominantly lethargy WITHOUT classic triad — 'medical mimic of sepsis' — requires high index of suspicion in this age group

labs.v: STRICT NPO since 0700 (vomiting + planned procedure); IV access (22 g R hand); NS 20 mL/kg bolus given; maintenance D5 1/2 NS at 42 mL/hr per Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 (= 40 + 2.4 mL/hr for 11.2 kg); ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg = 1.7 mg IV given for nausea (safe in peds; QT monitoring not routinely needed for single dose); pediatric surgery consulted at 0915, on standby; IR notified, ready by 1100; consent obtained from parents; child life specialist preparing the family + age-appropriate explanation
labs.r: Pre-procedure preparation: NPO, IV access, rehydration, antiemetic (ondansetron preferred — safe + effective; AVOID promethazine in <2 yr — FDA boxed warning for respiratory depression; metoclopramide alternative). Consent process per institutional protocol. Pediatric surgery on standby for failed reduction or perforation. Child life specialist for psychosocial support

labs.v: Previously healthy, full-term birth, no chronic conditions; immunizations up to date for age (DTaP, IPV, MMR, varicella, Hib, rotavirus, pneumococcal); breast-fed until 9 mo, transitioned to whole milk + table foods; 5-day URI 2 weeks ago (cough/rhinorrhea; resolved) — possible POST-VIRAL LYMPHOID HYPERPLASIA as lead point in this idiopathic age range; no recent abdominal trauma; no family history of GI disease; lives with parents + older sibling (age 4); no daycare exposure
labs.r: PMH context. The POST-VIRAL LYMPHOID HYPERPLASIA hypothesis (recent viral illness causes Peyer's patch enlargement → can serve as transient lead point for intussusception) is common in this idiopathic age range 3 mo–3 yr. ROTAVIRUS VACCINE has small association with intussusception (~1 in 50,000 — risk-benefit favors vaccination given prevention of severe gastroenteritis). Patient is vaccinated — slight risk but outweighed by benefit

labs.v: Other causes of intermittent severe abdominal pain + vomiting + bloody stool in toddler: (a) GASTROENTERITIS (would have diarrhea more than constipation, fever common, less pain severity); (b) APPENDICITIS (less common in <2 yr, persistent pain not intermittent, RLQ tenderness, fever); (c) MALROTATION WITH VOLVULUS (bilious vomiting in young infant, surgical emergency, UGI series diagnostic — must rule out in any bilious vomiting); (d) MECKEL'S DIVERTICULUM with bleeding (painless lower GI bleed typically, not the pattern here); (e) HENOCH-SCHÖNLEIN PURPURA (palpable purpura on legs + arthralgia + bloody stool — physical findings absent here); (f) HIRSCHSPRUNG (chronic constipation history); (g) PROTEIN-INDUCED ENTEROCOLITIS
labs.r: MALROTATION WITH VOLVULUS is the most important differential in any infant with bilious vomiting — surgical emergency, UGI series is diagnostic test (intussusception US is also test); often co-managed by peds surgery from start. Each differential has different presentation pattern; intussusception's intermittent severe pain + currant jelly + sausage mass + US findings are highly specific

labs.v: No fever >38°C (rules out frank septic component currently); no peritoneal signs (no rebound, no rigidity, no severe tenderness — rules out perforation currently); no free air on abdominal X-ray (rules out perforation); no signs of shock (HR elevated but BP maintained, mental status preserved); no significant abdominal trauma history; no hematemesis (rules out upper GI source)
labs.r: Pertinent negatives narrow the differential and inform treatment plan. NO perforation signs = air enema reduction is appropriate first-line; if any of these were present, surgical reduction would be required instead

nursesNote: 1030 — 14 mo (11.2 kg) previously-healthy male presented to peds ED at 0830 via parents for 18-hr history of intermittent severe abdominal pain (episodes of inconsolable crying with drawing knees to chest q20 min, separated by calm periods where child appears comfortable but lethargic), 8 episodes of vomiting (initially non-bilious, now BILIOUS), and at 0700 today passed BLOODY MUCUSY STOOL ('CURRANT JELLY' appearance — pathognomonic). CLASSIC TRIAD of INTUSSUSCEPTION present. Recent 5-day URI 2 weeks ago (post-viral lymphoid hyperplasia as likely lead point in this idiopathic age range 3 mo–3 yr). Physical exam between pain episodes: alert but lethargic, recognizes parents; SAUSAGE-SHAPED MASS palpable in RUQ (~3 cm — pathognomonic); RLQ feels 'empty' (Dance's sign — telescoped bowel pulled cephalad); abdomen mildly distended; mild tenderness with guarding during episodes, soft between; bowel sounds decreased; NO peritoneal signs (no rebound, no rigidity); rectal exam: mucusy + blood-tinged stool, no mass. Mild-moderate dehydration: cap refill 2–3 sec, dry mucous membranes, decreased tears, last wet diaper 5 hr ago. STAT BEDSIDE ULTRASOUND at 0900 by peds ED physician confirmed ILEOCECAL INTUSSUSCEPTION: 'TARGET SIGN' (axial concentric rings) + 'PSEUDOKIDNEY SIGN' (longitudinal) in RUQ; minimal free fluid; NO perforation; no obvious pathologic lead point (idiopathic — typical for age). Labs: Na 136, K 3.6, Cl 100, HCO3 22 (mild metabolic alkalosis from vomiting), glucose 88, Cr 0.4 (age-appropriate). PALS recommendation NS 20 mL/kg = 220 mL bolus given at 0900 for moderate dehydration (reassessed — perfusion improving); maintenance D5 1/2 NS at 42 mL/hr per Holliday-Segar 4-2-1; ONDANSETRON 0.15 mg/kg = 1.7 mg IV given for nausea (peds-safe; AVOID promethazine in <2 yr — FDA boxed warning for respiratory depression). NPO since 0700 in preparation for procedure. AIR ENEMA REDUCTION planned in IR at 1100 (preferred over hydrostatic contrast or barium enema; success rate 80–90%; less perforation risk than barium; barium AVOIDED if perforation possible due to chemical peritonitis risk); procedural sedation per peds anesthesia protocol; PEDIATRIC SURGERY consulted at 0915 + ON STANDBY for failed reduction or perforation; IR ready by 1100. SURGICAL REDUCTION indicated if (a) air enema fails after 2–3 attempts, (b) signs of perforation/peritonitis, (c) hemodynamic instability, (d) pathologic lead point identified (rare in this age), (e) bowel ischemia/necrosis requiring resection. Differential ruled out: MALROTATION WITH VOLVULUS (would have UGI series but US findings clearly intussusception; surgery alerted regardless of differential), appendicitis (less common in <2 yr, persistent not intermittent pain), gastroenteritis (less pain severity), Meckel (painless typically), HSP (no purpura/arthralgia). CONSENT obtained from parents (father — primary parent contact; mother at home with older sibling — coming as soon as possible); child life specialist providing age-appropriate explanation and family support. ANTICIPATED COURSE: air enema reduction success 80–90%; if successful, observation 12–24 hr NPO post-reduction for recurrence (5–10% recurrence rate), then advance diet as tolerated; if failed enema or perforation → OR for surgical reduction; PICU monitoring if perforation/sepsis develops. Discharge planning: parental education on recurrence signs (any recurrence of intermittent severe abdominal pain + vomiting + bloody stool → return immediately), follow-up with PCP within 1 week, peds surgery follow-up if needed. Family-centered care: parents present continuously; chaplain available; sibling care coordination through grandparents.
providerOrders: ADMIT to peds floor; pediatric surgery consultation (ON STANDBY for failed enema or perforation); pediatric GI/IR for air enema reduction; pediatric anesthesia for procedural sedation; child life specialist for psychosocial support
STRICT NPO since 0700; preparing for air enema reduction at 1100
WEIGHT 11.2 kg confirmed and documented as DOSING WEIGHT for all medications + fluids

Hydration: NS 20 mL/kg = 220 mL IV bolus over 20 min (PALS for moderate dehydration); reassess perfusion (capillary refill, mucous membranes, mental status, HR) after; repeat 20 mL/kg if continued dehydration up to 60 mL/kg in first hour (watch for fluid overload — hepatomegaly, crackles, worsening O2 needs); MAINTENANCE D5 1/2 NS at 42 mL/hr per Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 (= 40 + 2.4 mL/hr for 11.2 kg)

Antiemetic: ONDANSETRON 0.15 mg/kg = 1.7 mg IV q8h PRN nausea (peds-safe; QT monitoring not routinely needed for single dose unless concurrent QT-prolonging meds); AVOID promethazine in children <2 yr (FDA BLACK BOX WARNING for respiratory depression including death); metoclopramide alternative but less effective for nausea

AIR ENEMA REDUCTION in IR with continuous fluoroscopic monitoring; air insufflated rectally to pressure ~80–120 mmHg (institutional protocol); reduction success defined by air passing freely into terminal ileum; success rate 80–90%; perforation risk ~1%; pediatric surgery + anesthesia + transfusion bank on standby

HYDROSTATIC ENEMA (saline or water-soluble contrast like gastrografin) is alternative to air; equivalent efficacy; AVOID BARIUM if perforation possible (chemical peritonitis from extravasated barium → emergency surgery)
PROCEDURAL SEDATION per peds anesthesia protocol (e.g., midazolam + ketamine, or general anesthesia for high-risk cases); IV access maintained; continuous monitoring

If AIR ENEMA FAILS (after 2–3 attempts per institutional protocol) OR SIGNS OF PERFORATION DURING REDUCTION → EMERGENT TRANSFER TO OR for surgical reduction (laparoscopic or open); pediatric surgery primary; possible bowel resection if non-viable segment identified

SURGICAL REDUCTION indications (skip air enema, go directly to surgery): (a) PERITONITIS/PERFORATION at presentation, (b) HEMODYNAMIC INSTABILITY (shock), (c) PATHOLOGIC LEAD POINT identified (rare in this idiopathic age range), (d) BOWEL ISCHEMIA suspected; surgery may be open laparotomy or laparoscopic (institution-dependent)

Continuous monitoring: HR, BP, SpO2, RR; q15-min vitals during active resuscitation + procedure then q1–2h; foley for accurate UOP if hemodynamically unstable (otherwise weigh diapers); pain assessment with FLACC (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability) scale q4h

STRICT NPO post-reduction for 12–24 hr observation period; advance diet (clear liquids → regular formula/diet) as tolerated if no recurrence signs; recurrence rate ~5–10% after successful reduction (often within 24–48 hr)
Parental education for recurrence recognition: any return of intermittent severe abdominal pain + vomiting + bloody stool → return to ED IMMEDIATELY; signs of recurrence (rare but high-yield)

Pain management: ACETAMINOPHEN 15 mg/kg = 168 mg PO/PR q6h PRN pain (scheduled if pain ongoing); morphine 0.05–0.1 mg/kg IV PRN severe pain (use cautiously in peds — respiratory depression risk; avoid masking peritoneal signs that would warrant surgery)
Trend labs q4h initially: BMP (electrolyte derangement from vomiting/dehydration), CBC (leukocytosis suggests inflammation/infection), lactate (if signs of sepsis), coags
Pediatric surgery + IR + anesthesia coordination; PICU bed available if perforation/sepsis develops

Family-centered care: parents continuously present; child life specialist for age-appropriate explanation + distraction during procedure; chaplain available; coordinate sibling care through grandparents; lactation/feeding support post-reduction; emotional support throughout
Discharge criteria: successful reduction + 12–24 hr observation NPO without recurrence + tolerating PO + adequate UOP + parents demonstrating recurrence recognition + follow-up scheduled

Outpatient follow-up: PCP within 1 week of discharge; peds surgery if surgical reduction was performed; reassessment for any concerning symptoms; immunizations on schedule (no special considerations after intussusception); discuss rotavirus vaccine if any future siblings being immunized (very small association with intussusception ~1 in 50,000 — risk-benefit still favors vaccination but worth informed discussion)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: CLASSIC TRIAD of intussusception: INTERMITTENT SEVERE pain (drawing knees up, calm intervals) + BILIOUS vomiting + CURRANT-JELLY stool (blood + mucus from sloughed mucosa)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic recognition. Classic triad present in only ~20% of cases (partial more common); when full triad present = highly suggestive of intussusception. Bilious vomiting indicates obstruction. Currant jelly is a LATE sign of bowel ischemia.

opts.text: Bedside abdominal ULTRASOUND confirmed ILEOCECAL intussusception with 'TARGET SIGN' (axial concentric rings) + 'PSEUDOKIDNEY SIGN' (longitudinal); no perforation; no obvious pathologic lead point
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic test of choice. US is first-line (sensitivity + specificity >97%; non-invasive; no radiation). Findings are characteristic. Confirms ileocecal location (most common in children, ~90%). Absence of perforation supports air enema reduction attempt.

opts.text: SAUSAGE-SHAPED MASS palpable in RUQ + DANCE'S SIGN (empty RLQ from telescoped bowel pulled cephalad) — pathognomonic when present
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — physical exam findings. Mass palpable in only ~50% of cases; when present, highly suggestive. Dance's sign reflects displacement of bowel. NO peritoneal signs (rebound, rigidity) = no perforation currently — supports air enema trial.

opts.text: AIR ENEMA REDUCTION planned (success rate 80–90%); peds surgery + anesthesia + IR coordination; ON STANDBY for failed reduction or perforation; barium AVOIDED if perforation possible
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — definitive treatment first-line. Air enema preferred over hydrostatic (saline/contrast) over barium. Barium avoidance is critical (chemical peritonitis if perforation). Procedural sedation per peds anesthesia. Surgery on standby.

opts.text: Indications for SURGICAL reduction (not air enema): peritonitis/perforation at presentation, hemodynamic instability, pathologic lead point identified, bowel ischemia, failed enema after 2–3 attempts
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — escalation criteria. Recognize when air enema is contraindicated or unsafe. Peritoneal signs change the entire management. Pathologic lead point (Meckel, polyp, lymphoma) more common in older children (>5 yr) or recurrent intussusception.

opts.text: Mild-moderate dehydration: cap refill 2–3 sec, dry mucous membranes, decreased tears, last wet diaper 5 hr ago; PALS NS 20 mL/kg bolus given (reassess + repeat up to 60 mL/kg in first hour if needed)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — dehydration assessment in peds. Use capillary refill (most sensitive in peds), mucous membranes, tears, fontanelle, skin turgor, mental status, UOP. PALS bolus 20 mL/kg over 10–20 min. Reassess; repeat if needed up to 60 mL/kg. Watch for fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles).

opts.text: Weight 11.2 kg confirmed as DOSING WEIGHT for all peds medications + fluids; Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 = 42 mL/hr maintenance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pediatric dosing principle. EVERY drug + fluid is mg/kg or mL/kg. Confirm at admission. Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 for maintenance fluids (4 mL/kg/hr first 10 kg + 2 mL/kg/hr next 10 kg + 1 mL/kg/hr beyond).

opts.text: Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg = 1.7 mg IV for nausea; AVOID PROMETHAZINE in <2 yr (FDA BLACK BOX WARNING for respiratory depression including death)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pediatric medication safety. Ondansetron preferred + safe (single-dose QT monitoring usually not needed). Promethazine BLACK BOX in <2 yr. Metoclopramide alternative but less effective. AVOID DIPHENHYDRAMINE in elderly (Beers) — but that's age-different concern.

opts.text: Pediatric pain assessment: FLACC scale (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability) q4h; child cannot use NRS (numeric rating scale) at age 14 mo
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — age-appropriate pain assessment. FLACC scale (0–10) for pre-verbal children (0–7 years typically). Faces scale (FACES, 3–18 yr). NRS (0–10) for older children + adolescents who can use numbers. PAINAD (pain assessment in advanced dementia) for confused elderly. Match scale to patient capability.

opts.text: Parents continuously present; child life specialist providing age-appropriate explanation + family support
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — family-centered pediatric care. Parents at bedside reduce child's distress (familiar face). Child life specialist supports both child + family. Distraction techniques (toys, age-appropriate explanation) during procedures. Cultural + linguistic considerations.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed.

opts.text: Temperature 37.4°C (within normal — no fever)
opts.rat: Reassuring — rules out frank septic component currently. Fever would raise concern for perforation/peritonitis/sepsis. Continue to monitor.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology / pediatric principles.

opts.text: INTUSSUSCEPTION pathophysiology: PROXIMAL bowel telescoping INTO DISTAL bowel = ILEOCECAL most common in children (~90%); intussusceptum drags mesentery → vascular compression → bowel wall edema/congestion → mucosal ischemia (sloughs → blood + mucus = CURRANT JELLY) → can progress to full-thickness necrosis → PERFORATION → peritonitis → sepsis
opts.rat: CORRECT — central mechanism. Time-sensitive: earlier reduction = higher success of enema + lower perforation risk. >48 hr or signs of perforation/sepsis = surgical reduction with possible bowel resection. The intermittent pain pattern corresponds to peristaltic waves through the affected segment.

opts.text: CLASSIC TRIAD of intussusception: INTERMITTENT SEVERE pain (drawing knees up, calm intervals) + VOMITING (initially non-bilious then BILIOUS as obstruction progresses) + CURRANT-JELLY STOOL (blood + mucus); full triad in only ~20% of cases; partial presentations more common; INTUSSUSCEPTION LETHARGY can be sole presenting feature in some cases (mimic of sepsis)
opts.rat: CORRECT — clinical recognition. Partial presentations are easy to miss. Lethargy alone in this age group should prompt intussusception consideration. Bilious vomiting indicates obstruction (vs simple gastroenteritis with non-bilious). Currant jelly is a LATE sign.

opts.text: DIAGNOSIS: ABDOMINAL ULTRASOUND is FIRST-LINE (sensitivity + specificity >97%; non-invasive; no radiation); 'TARGET SIGN' on axial view (concentric rings) + 'PSEUDOKIDNEY SIGN' on longitudinal view; abdominal X-ray less sensitive (~50%) but can show paucity of gas RLQ, soft tissue mass, signs of obstruction or free air (if perforated)
opts.rat: CORRECT — diagnostic approach. US has displaced X-ray as first-line for peds intussusception. CT for older children or atypical cases. UGI series for malrotation suspicion (the most important differential in bilious vomiting infants).

opts.text: TREATMENT: AIR ENEMA REDUCTION first-line (success rate 80–90%; perforation risk ~1%); HYDROSTATIC enema (saline or water-soluble contrast) alternative; BARIUM AVOIDED if perforation possible (chemical peritonitis from extravasated barium); SURGICAL REDUCTION for failed enema OR peritonitis/perforation OR hemodynamic instability OR pathologic lead point OR bowel ischemia
opts.rat: CORRECT — treatment hierarchy. Air enema preferred over barium (safer if perforation). Pediatric surgery on standby for any reduction. Surgical reduction may involve bowel resection if non-viable segment.

opts.text: PATHOLOGIC LEAD POINT consideration: most common in <2 yr is IDIOPATHIC (post-viral lymphoid hyperplasia = Peyer's patch enlargement as transient lead point); pathologic lead points (MECKEL DIVERTICULUM, polyps, lymphoma, intramural hematoma from HSP, tumors) MORE COMMON in older children (>5 yr) or recurrent intussusception — warrants thorough workup
opts.rat: CORRECT — age-stratified consideration. The 3 mo–3 yr age range = idiopathic predominant. Older children or recurrent cases = think pathologic lead point. Meckel diverticulum (rule of 2's: 2% of population, 2 ft from ileocecal valve, 2 inches long, present <2 yr typically, M:F 2:1) is classic. Workup: enema reduction with attention for lead point; if recurrent or older child, consider laparoscopic exploration.

opts.text: PEDIATRIC FLUID RESUSCITATION per PALS: 20 mL/kg ISOTONIC bolus (NS or LR) over 10–20 min for moderate-severe dehydration; REASSESS perfusion (cap refill, mucous membranes, mental status, HR, UOP) after each bolus; repeat up to 60 mL/kg in first hour if needed; MAINTENANCE D5 1/2 NS (or isotonic D5 NS per recent guidance) per HOLLIDAY-SEGAR 4-2-1 RULE (4 mL/kg/hr first 10 kg + 2 mL/kg/hr next 10 kg + 1 mL/kg/hr beyond)
opts.rat: CORRECT — pediatric fluid fundamentals. PALS algorithm. Holliday-Segar 4-2-1 for maintenance. Recent guidance favors isotonic D5 NS over D5 1/2 NS to prevent hospital-acquired hyponatremia in acutely ill children. Watch for fluid overload (hepatomegaly, crackles, worsening O2 needs).

opts.text: PEDIATRIC ANTIEMETIC SAFETY: ONDANSETRON 0.15 mg/kg IV (preferred — safe + effective; QT monitoring not needed for single dose unless concurrent QT-prolonging meds); ⚠️ PROMETHAZINE FDA BLACK BOX WARNING for respiratory depression in children <2 yr (including death); METOCLOPRAMIDE alternative but less effective + risk of EPS
opts.rat: CORRECT — peds medication safety. Promethazine BLACK BOX in <2 yr is high-yield. Other peds-safe antiemetics: ondansetron (any age), metoclopramide (with caution for EPS), dimenhydrinate (>2 yr). AVOID promethazine, prochlorperazine in young children.

opts.text: Recent VIRAL ILLNESS (URI, gastroenteritis) is associated with intussusception in children — hypothesis: viral-induced LYMPHOID HYPERPLASIA in Peyer's patches creates transient lead point; ROTAVIRUS VACCINE has small association with intussusception (~1 in 50,000) — risk-benefit FAVORS vaccination given prevention of severe gastroenteritis
opts.rat: CORRECT — epidemiology. Recent viral illness is a recognized trigger. Rotavirus vaccine association is real but small; the benefit of preventing severe rotavirus gastroenteritis (hospitalization, death) outweighs the small intussusception risk. Counsel families on benefits + risks; do not avoid vaccination based on this small risk.

opts.text: Use BARIUM ENEMA first-line for intussusception reduction — it provides better visualization than air enema
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — outdated and unsafe. Barium AVOIDED if perforation possible (chemical peritonitis from extravasated barium → emergency surgery). AIR ENEMA is current first-line (equivalent efficacy, much safer if perforation occurs). Hydrostatic (saline/water-soluble contrast like gastrografin) is alternative.

opts.text: Give PROMETHAZINE 12.5 mg IV for nausea in this 14-month-old
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG — FDA BLACK BOX WARNING for respiratory depression in children <2 yr. Use ONDANSETRON instead (peds-safe at all ages). This is a tested pediatric medication safety point.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Bowel Ischemia / Perforation / Sepsis r/t intussusception with vascular compromise; time-sensitive intervention required
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The longer the intussusception remains, the more bowel ischemia. Time-sensitive: <24 hr from onset = higher enema reduction success + lower perforation risk. Patient at 18 hr — within window. Air enema reduction is the definitive treatment.

opts.text: Dehydration / Electrolyte Imbalance r/t bilious vomiting + reduced PO intake + bowel obstruction
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Moderate dehydration requires PALS NS 20 mL/kg bolus + maintenance. Electrolyte derangement (hypochloremic alkalosis from vomiting) typically present. Trend BMP; replace as needed. Watch for fluid overload in pediatric resuscitation.

opts.text: Risk for Air Enema Complications r/t reduction procedure (perforation ~1%, failed reduction in 10–20%); need for surgical backup
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Procedure-related risks. Pediatric surgery + anesthesia on standby. OR ready. Continuous monitoring during procedure. Conversion to surgical reduction if needed.

opts.text: Acute Pain r/t intussusception with intermittent severe episodes; PAINAD/FLACC pain assessment, acetaminophen scheduled + morphine PRN; AVOID masking peritoneal signs
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Adequate pain control for child's comfort + family distress reduction. Use FLACC scale (pre-verbal child). Acetaminophen + judicious morphine (low dose; respiratory depression risk in peds). Avoid heavy opioids that mask peritoneal signs warranting surgery.

opts.text: Family Distress / Knowledge Deficit r/t acute illness in toddler, planned procedure, recurrence risk, family-centered care needs
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest in immediate physiologic stack. Parents continuously at bedside; child life specialist; chaplain; sibling care coordination; age-appropriate explanation; teaching for recurrence signs at discharge.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Strict NPO; IV access; NS 20 mL/kg bolus (PALS for moderate dehydration); reassess + repeat up to 60 mL/kg in first hour if needed; maintenance D5 1/2 NS at 42 mL/hr per Holliday-Segar
opts.rat: Standard pediatric fluid resuscitation + maintenance. PALS bolus protocol. Reassess between boluses (watch for fluid overload). Holliday-Segar for maintenance.

opts.text: ONDANSETRON 0.15 mg/kg = 1.7 mg IV for nausea (peds-safe at all ages)
opts.rat: Preferred peds antiemetic. Safe + effective. QT monitoring not needed for single dose. Avoid promethazine in <2 yr (BLACK BOX).

opts.text: PROMETHAZINE 12.5 mg IV for nausea in this 14-month-old
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS — FDA BLACK BOX WARNING for respiratory depression including death in children <2 yr. Use ondansetron. Common peds medication safety point.

opts.text: AIR ENEMA REDUCTION in IR with continuous fluoroscopic monitoring; air insufflated rectally to ~80–120 mmHg; peds surgery + anesthesia on standby; procedural sedation per peds anesthesia protocol
opts.rat: First-line definitive treatment. Success rate 80–90%. Lower perforation risk than barium. Surgical backup essential. Procedural sedation per institutional + patient-specific protocol.

opts.text: BARIUM ENEMA reduction as first-line (better visualization than air)
opts.rat: ⚠️ Outdated + unsafe. Barium AVOIDED if perforation possible (chemical peritonitis from extravasated barium). Air enema is current first-line (equivalent efficacy, much safer). Hydrostatic with water-soluble contrast (gastrografin) is alternative to air.

opts.text: SURGICAL REDUCTION (laparoscopic or open) if air enema fails after 2–3 attempts, OR signs of perforation/peritonitis, OR hemodynamic instability, OR pathologic lead point, OR bowel ischemia/necrosis
opts.rat: Surgical reduction indications. Open or laparoscopic per institution. Manual reduction first; resection if non-viable bowel; primary anastomosis usually possible if limited.

opts.text: Post-reduction observation NPO 12–24 hr; advance diet (clear liquids → regular formula/diet) as tolerated if no recurrence signs; recurrence rate ~5–10% (often within 24–48 hr)
opts.rat: Standard post-reduction protocol. NPO observation watches for early recurrence. Advance diet gradually. Recurrence signs: any return of intermittent severe abdominal pain + vomiting + bloody stool — return immediately.

opts.text: Pain management with ACETAMINOPHEN 15 mg/kg = 168 mg PO/PR q6h (scheduled or PRN); MORPHINE 0.05–0.1 mg/kg IV PRN severe pain (cautiously — respiratory depression risk in peds, avoid masking peritoneal signs)
opts.rat: Multimodal pain management. Scheduled acetaminophen baseline. Low-dose morphine for breakthrough. Avoid heavy opioids that mask peritoneal signs warranting surgery. FLACC scale for assessment in pre-verbal child.

opts.text: Pediatric surgery + IR + anesthesia coordination throughout; PICU bed available if perforation/sepsis develops
opts.rat: Multidisciplinary coordination. Standard for pediatric surgical emergencies. Surgical backup essential for any reduction. PICU for complications.

opts.text: Child life specialist for age-appropriate explanation + distraction during procedures; parents continuously at bedside; family-centered care; sibling care coordination through grandparents
opts.rat: Pediatric family-centered care standard. Reduces child + family distress. Child life specialist invaluable for procedural support. Parents at bedside reduce separation anxiety. Address family needs (sibling care, lodging, food).

opts.text: Discharge education: any recurrence of intermittent severe abdominal pain + vomiting + bloody stool → return IMMEDIATELY; rotavirus vaccine discussion if any future siblings (small association ~1 in 50,000 — risk-benefit favors vaccination)
opts.rat: Recurrence rate 5–10%; family must recognize warning signs. Most recurrences within 24–48 hr but can be later. Outpatient follow-up scheduled. Rotavirus vaccine discussion informed.

opts.text: Discharge home immediately after successful air enema reduction without observation period
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Post-reduction observation 12–24 hr NPO is standard to watch for early recurrence (most within 24–48 hr but early recurrence within hours of reduction is documented). Advance diet gradually with monitoring. Discharge after observation + tolerating PO + parents demonstrating recurrence recognition.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm weight 11.2 kg + establish IV access + STAT bedside abdominal ULTRASOUND to confirm intussusception; STRICT NPO; FLACC pain assessment
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Weight for dosing, IV for resuscitation, US for diagnosis. Strict NPO essential for upcoming procedure. Pain assessment with age-appropriate scale (FLACC for pre-verbal). All occur in first 30 min.

opts.text: NS 20 mL/kg = 220 mL IV bolus over 20 min (PALS for moderate dehydration); reassess; ONDANSETRON 0.15 mg/kg = 1.7 mg IV for nausea; maintenance D5 1/2 NS at 42 mL/hr per Holliday-Segar
opts.rat: #2 — Hydration + symptom control. PALS bolus protocol with reassessment. Ondansetron preferred (AVOID promethazine in <2 yr — BLACK BOX). Maintenance per Holliday-Segar 4-2-1.

opts.text: Pediatric surgery + IR + anesthesia consultations; coordinate AIR ENEMA reduction in IR; surgery on STANDBY for failed reduction or perforation; procedural sedation per peds anesthesia
opts.rat: #3 — Definitive treatment coordination. Air enema first-line (80–90% success, ~1% perforation risk). Multidisciplinary coordination. Procedural sedation per anesthesia. OR ready as backup.

opts.text: Post-reduction observation NPO 12–24 hr on peds floor; advance diet (clears → formula/diet) as tolerated; trend labs; FLACC pain assessment; continued IV fluids; monitor for recurrence signs
opts.rat: #4 — Post-procedure care. Standard observation period. Watch for early recurrence (most within 24–48 hr). Advance diet gradually. Pain control. Continued monitoring.

opts.text: Discharge planning: parental education on recurrence signs (intermittent pain + vomiting + bloody stool → ED immediately); PCP follow-up within 1 week; family-centered care + sibling coordination + chaplain support
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained care + family. Recurrence rate 5–10%; family must recognize. Outpatient follow-up structure. Family + sibling care coordination. Chaplain support throughout.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Air enema reduction completed successfully at 1130; fluoroscopic confirmation of air freely passing into terminal ileum (intussusception reduced); patient stable through procedure; post-reduction US shows no residual intussusception
opts.rat: Successful reduction. Continue post-reduction observation NPO 12–24 hr. Advance diet gradually. Monitor for early recurrence (most within 24–48 hr). Parental education on recurrence signs.

opts.text: Day 1 evening: tolerating clear liquids without recurrence; passing normal stool; pain resolved (FLACC 0); ambulating with parents (cruising furniture, age-appropriate); plan discharge tomorrow if continues without symptoms
opts.rat: Excellent post-reduction recovery. Continue advance of diet to regular formula/diet. Discharge planning. Parents demonstrating recurrence recognition. Follow-up scheduled.

opts.text: Discharge day 2: tolerating regular diet, normal stool, no pain, parents verbalize understanding of recurrence signs (intermittent pain + vomiting + bloody stool → return ED), PCP follow-up scheduled within 1 week, family-centered care addressed (chaplain + sibling care + family questions answered); rotavirus vaccine discussion for future planning
opts.rat: Successful discharge. Family empowered. Comprehensive education completed. Outpatient follow-up structure. Most cases resolve without recurrence; vigilance maintained.

opts.text: During air enema: sudden bowel perforation with free air on fluoroscopy; emergent transfer to OR for surgical reduction + bowel resection of necrotic segment; primary anastomosis performed
opts.rat: Procedure complication (~1% perforation risk). Emergent surgical management. Pediatric surgery primary. Resection of non-viable bowel + anastomosis. Post-op PICU monitoring; antibiotics; NPO; NG decompression; expected longer recovery. Family meeting + chaplain support.

opts.text: 12 hr post-reduction: return of intermittent severe abdominal pain + vomiting; repeat US shows RECURRENT intussusception; second air enema reduction attempt successful
opts.rat: Early recurrence (5–10% rate; most within 24–48 hr). Repeat reduction attempt appropriate. If multiple recurrences, consider workup for pathologic lead point (Meckel diverticulum, polyp, lymphoma) — more common in older children but worth investigating with imaging or surgical exploration.

opts.text: Day 1: develops new fever 39.2°C + worsening abdominal distention + new peritoneal signs (rigidity, guarding); CBC shows WBC 22 with bandemia; lactate 3.4 — late perforation or bowel ischemia missed at initial reduction
opts.rat: Bowel ischemia/perforation developing. Emergent surgical exploration; broad-spectrum antibiotics; PICU; fluid resuscitation; possible bowel resection. Mortality higher with late presentation/perforation. Family meeting.

opts.text: 3-month outpatient follow-up: well, growing appropriately, no recurrence, eating normally, family verbalize ongoing recognition of recurrence signs; PCP routine well-child care + immunizations on schedule
opts.rat: Best-case long-term outcome. No recurrence. Routine pediatric care resumed. Family informed advocates. Immunizations continue on schedule. Most idiopathic intussusception in young children does not recur after successful reduction.

opts.text: After air enema reduction, the radiologist gave promethazine 6.25 mg IV for post-procedure nausea despite chart alert about age <2 yr; pediatric nurse intercepted, switched to ondansetron 1.7 mg IV; FDA black box warning documented; near-miss reporting
opts.rat: System safety win. Nurse appropriately intercepted contraindicated medication. Promethazine BLACK BOX in <2 yr. Education for radiologist; chart alert verified active; near-miss documentation for system learning. Ondansetron safer alternative.

opts.text: Persistent mild fatigue and decreased appetite at 1-week PCP follow-up; otherwise well; gradually returning to baseline activity and feeding
opts.rat: Expected slow recovery after acute illness in toddler. Continued recovery of appetite + energy over 1–2 weeks. Reassure family; encourage hydration + favorite foods; routine pediatric follow-up; no special intervention needed if no concerning symptoms.

opts.text: Family verbalizes anxiety about future possible recurrence; child life + social work provide ongoing support resources; written recurrence-signs education provided; 24/7 phone access to peds advice line confirmed
opts.rat: Expected family anxiety after acute pediatric illness. Validate; provide resources; written education for reference; 24/7 advice line access; reinforce that most cases do not recur; PCP follow-up structured. Family-centered care extends beyond hospitalization.
